topic: Aspects of French-speaking Society: Current Trends
subject: french

items.q: When does the past participle agree with avoir verbs?
items.correct: When the direct object comes BEFORE the verb (la pomme que j'ai mangée).
items.why: Students learn 'avoir = no agreement' as a blanket rule. The exception is PRECEDING DIRECT OBJECT: 'Je l'ai mangée' (la pomme) requires the participle to agree with the object pronoun.
A classic faux ami. Actuellement looks like 'actually' but means CURRENTLY. To say 'actually' use en fait or en réalité. These false friends regularly cost marks.

items.q: Where does 'petit' usually go in relation to its noun?
items.misconception: After the noun like most French adjectives.
items.random_wrong: Either position with the same meaning.
items.why: Students apply 'all French adjectives follow nouns' too rigidly. BAGS adjectives (Beauty, Age, Goodness, Size) typically PRECEDE. Petit (size) and bon (goodness) come before.

items.q: What is the correct future tense of 'changer' for 'il'?
items.why: It's changera. “changerait” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many députés sit in the Assemblée nationale?
items.why: Correct answer: 577. “348” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year was the Fifth Republic established?
items.why: The real figure is 1958. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
